section.title: What is a differential form?
section.p: A curve gives tiny tangent arrows. A surface gives tiny oriented parallelograms. A solid gives tiny oriented boxes. A differential form is a rule that knows how to measure one of those tiny objects.

subsection.title: Forms as objects designed to be integrated
subsection.p: Think of four measurements in a small laboratory.

A temperature reading at one point is just a number: T(P). A force measurement along a wire uses tiny displacements: P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz. A flow measurement through a screen uses tiny oriented area pieces: P\,dy\wedge dz+Q\,dz\wedge dx+R\,dx\wedge dy. A density measurement throughout a box uses tiny volume pieces: \rho\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz.
Each expression is designed for a different dimension.
The degree tells us the dimension of the object the form measures.

figure.caption: A k-form is built to be integrated over k-dimensional oriented objects.

subsection.p: This is the guiding idea: \boxed{\text{A differential form is an object designed to be integrated.}}
The symbols dx, dy, dz, and their wedge products are not decorations. They tell the form what kind of tiny geometric piece it is measuring.

subsection.title: 0-forms: functions
subsection.p: A 0-form is a function.
For example, on the real line, f(x)=x^2 is a 0-form. On the plane, T(x,y)=20+\frac{6}{1+(x-2)^2+(y-1)^2} is also a 0-form. It assigns one number to each point.

The unusual part is integration. A 0-form is integrated over oriented points. An oriented point is just a point with a sign attached: +P \qquad\text{or}\qquad -P. We define \int_{+P} f=f(P), \qquad \int_{-P} f=-f(P).

This may look strange until we remember the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. The oriented boundary of the interval [a,b] is \partial[a,b]=\{b\}-\{a\}. So \int_{\partial[a,b]} f = f(b)-f(a). That is exactly the boundary term in \int_a^b f'(x)\,dx=f(b)-f(a).

example.title: A function on the boundary of an interval

statement.p:
Let f(x)=x^2+1 and let I=[1,4]. Then \partial I=\{4\}-\{1\}. Therefore
\int_{\partial I} f \amp= f(4)-f(1) \amp= 17-2 \amp= 15.
The signs on the endpoints matter. The right endpoint is positive, and the left endpoint is negative.

subsection.p: This is why functions belong in the forms family. They are the forms of degree zero. They do not measure length, area, or volume. They measure signed points.

statement.p: A 0-form on a region D is a scalar function f:D\to\mathbb R. It is integrated over oriented 0-dimensional objects, which are signed collections of points.

subsection.p: Later, the exterior derivative will turn a 0-form into a 1-form: f \quad\longmapsto\quad df. That is the form version of taking a derivative.

subsection.title: 1-forms: line-measuring objects
subsection.p: A 1-form measures one tangent direction at a time. That is why it is integrated along a curve.

The work form of a vector field \mathbf F=(P,Q,R) is \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz. At each tiny step along a curve, it adds P(\text{tiny }x\text{-change}) + Q(\text{tiny }y\text{-change}) + R(\text{tiny }z\text{-change}).

example.title: A turnstile that charges by displacement

statement.p: A robot moves across a warehouse floor. The cost of a tiny move is \omega=3\,dx+2\,dy. Moving east by one meter costs 3 units. Moving north by one meter costs 2 units. Moving west or south gives a negative cost, because the displacement is oriented.

Let the robot move from (0,0) to (4,1) along the straight path \mathbf r(t)=(4t,t), \qquad 0\le t\le1. Then dx=4\,dt, \qquad dy=dt. So
\int_C \omega \amp= \int_0^1 \bigl(3(4\,dt)+2(dt)\bigr) \amp= \int_0^1 14\,dt \amp= 14.
The 1-form did not measure how long the path was. It measured the oriented coordinate changes along the path.

A 1-form on a region D\subseteq\mathbb R^2 is an expression \omega=P(x,y)\,dx+Q(x,y)\,dy. A 1-form on a region D\subseteq\mathbb R^3 is an expression \omega=P(x,y,z)\,dx+Q(x,y,z)\,dy+R(x,y,z)\,dz. It is integrated over oriented curves.

subsection.p: A 1-form changes sign when the orientation of the curve is reversed: \int_{-C}\omega=-\int_C\omega. That sign is part of the object. A 1-form is not an unsigned length density like \rho\,ds. The form P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz cares which way the curve is traveled.

subsection.title: 2-forms: surface-measuring objects
subsection.p: A 2-form measures an oriented area piece. In the plane, the basic area form is dx\wedge dy. So a typical 2-form in the plane is \alpha=M(x,y)\,dx\wedge dy.
On a positively oriented region in the xy-plane, this acts like M\,dA. But the wedge notation remembers the sign: dy\wedge dx=-dx\wedge dy.

example.title: A signed paint layer on a floor tile

statement.p:
Let \alpha=(2+x)\,dx\wedge dy on the rectangle R=[0,3]\times[0,2], with the usual orientation of the plane. Then
\int_R \alpha \amp= \int_0^3\int_0^2 (2+x)\,dy\,dx \amp= \int_0^3 2(2+x)\,dx \amp= \left[4x+x^2\right]_0^3 \amp= 21.
If we reverse the orientation of R, the same form gives -21. The physical amount of paint would not become negative, but the oriented integral does. A 2-form measures signed area.

subsection.p: In space, a surface can have shadows on three coordinate planes. The basic 2-forms are dy\wedge dz,\qquad dz\wedge dx,\qquad dx\wedge dy. A general 2-form in space has the shape \Phi=A\,dy\wedge dz+B\,dz\wedge dx+C\,dx\wedge dy.
Flux forms are the main example: \Phi_{\mathbf F} = P\,dy\wedge dz + Q\,dz\wedge dx + R\,dx\wedge dy for a vector field \mathbf F=(P,Q,R).

statement.p:
Let \Phi=5\,dy\wedge dz+2\,dz\wedge dx. Take the screen x=1, \qquad 0\le y\le2, \qquad 0\le z\le3, oriented in the positive x-direction. A parametrization is \mathbf r(y,z)=(1,y,z). On this screen, dy\wedge dz is the positive area form. Also, dx=0, so dz\wedge dx=0. Thus \mathbf r^*\Phi=5\,dy\wedge dz. Therefore
\int_S \Phi \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^3 5\,dz\,dy \amp= 30.
Only the dy\wedge dz part crosses a screen perpendicular to the x-axis.

A 2-form on a region in the plane has the form \alpha=M(x,y)\,dx\wedge dy. A 2-form on a region in space has the form \Phi=A\,dy\wedge dz+B\,dz\wedge dx+C\,dx\wedge dy. It is integrated over oriented surfaces or oriented plane regions.

subsection.p: Surface area integrals such as \iint_S f\,dS do not need an orientation. A 2-form integral does. That is why flux is the natural surface integral for forms.

subsection.title: 3-forms: volume-measuring objects
subsection.p: A 3-form measures oriented volume in space. The basic volume form is dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. A typical 3-form is \Omega=H(x,y,z)\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz.

statement.p:
A gas fills the box E=[0,2]\times[0,1]\times[0,3]. Suppose its density form is \Omega=(5+z)\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. Using the usual orientation of space,
\int_E \Omega \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^1\int_0^3 (5+z)\,dz\,dy\,dx \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^1 \left[5z+\frac{z^2}{2}\right]_0^3 \,dy\,dx \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^1 \frac{39}{2} \,dy\,dx \amp= 39.
So the total mass is 39. If we reverse the orientation of space, the form integral becomes -39. For physical mass we usually keep the standard orientation fixed and report the positive number.
A 3-form on a region in space is an expression \Omega=H(x,y,z)\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. It is integrated over oriented solid regions.

subsection.p: The whole family now fits into one picture:
subsection.md: \begin{array}{c|c|c|c} \text{Degree} \amp \text{Typical form} \amp \text{Tiny object measured} \amp \text{Integral} \\ \hline 0 \amp f \amp \text{oriented point} \amp \int_{\partial I} f\\[1mm] 1 \amp P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz \amp \text{oriented tangent vector} \amp \int_C \omega\\[1mm] 2 \amp A\,dy\wedge dz+B\,dz\wedge dx+C\,dx\wedge dy \amp \text{oriented parallelogram} \amp \int_S \Phi\\[1mm] 3 \amp H\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz \amp \text{oriented box} \amp \int_E \Omega \end{array}
subsection.p: The table hides one important question. A 1-form is built from dx, dy, and dz. But what are these symbols really? They are not tiny numbers. They are linear measuring rules for tangent vectors. Giving those rules their proper name, covectors, is the next step.
